(ii) The Loan Term Sheet (Section 6.3) requires the Construction Lender's prior written consent to any subordinate financing, and the Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 7(a)) requires Investor consent for Project-level indebtedness exceeding $268,400,000 in the aggregate ($210M Construction Loan + $58.4M additional). (iii) The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 5) grants Lakeshore preemptive rights, anti-dilution adjustments, and information rights in connection with any new equity issuance for later phases, and provides that dilution of the Pellegrini Interest below 51% constitutes a Change of Control under the Side Letter. These constraints must be harmonized with the assignment and change-of-control provisions of this Agreement (Section 16) and the Loan Documents. (iv) TransitWorks Equity Group LLC (15% member) is not contributing equity to the Project; confirm whether TransitWorks is expected to contribute to later phases.]

**6.5 Developer Financial Capacity.** Developer represents that its entity-level net worth is approximately $185,000,000 (as reflected in audited financial statements prepared by Clearmont Accounting Group LLP) and that it maintains bonding capacity of up to $75,000,000 through Ridgepoint Surety Corporation.

## ARTICLE 7. PERFORMANCE SECURITY

**7.1 Performance Bond or Letter of Credit.** As a Condition Precedent to Closing, Developer shall post a $15,000,000 performance bond (to be issued by Ridgepoint Surety Corporation or another surety company reasonably acceptable to the City) or an irrevocable standby letter of credit issued by a nationally recognized financial institution reasonably acceptable to the City (the "Performance Security"). The Performance Security shall secure Developer's obligation to commence and substantially complete Phase I in accordance with the approved plans, specifications, and timeline.

**7.2 Term; Release.** The Performance Security shall be maintained continuously from the date of Closing until Phase I Substantial Completion, as evidenced by issuance of a temporary or final CO by the City. Upon Phase I Substantial Completion, the Performance Security shall be released and returned to Developer within thirty (30) days following written request and verification of Substantial Completion by the City.

**7.3 Form and Draw Mechanics.** The amount, form, conditions, and draw mechanics of the Performance Security shall be as set forth in Exhibit E, in form and substance reasonably acceptable to the City and the Construction Lender.

[Drafting Note — Cross-document conflict (dual claimants on Performance Security): The Term Sheet and the Resolution require the $15,000,000 Performance Security to secure Developer's Phase I completion obligation to the City. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 4.1) requires assignment of the Performance Security (or an equivalent letter of credit) to the Construction Lender as collateral for the Construction Loan. Both the City and the Construction Lender assert interests in the same $15,000,000 instrument. This conflict must be resolved before Closing: options include (i) two separate instruments (one for the City, one for the Lender), (ii) a single instrument with an agreed draw priority and an intercreditor arrangement, or (iii) a single letter of credit with the City as beneficiary and a separate collateral assignment to the Lender. The draw priority and the consequences of competing draws must be specified in Exhibit E and any intercreditor agreement. Negotiate with the City and the Construction Lender.]

## ARTICLE 8. CONSTRUCTION STANDARDS

**8.1 Prevailing Wage.** All construction performed on the Project shall comply with the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act, 820 ILCS 130/0.01 et seq. Developer shall include prevailing wage requirements in all contracts and subcontracts for construction work performed in connection with the Project. Developer shall maintain certified payroll records for all construction workers employed on the Project and shall make such records available to the City and to the Illinois Department of Labor upon request. Developer shall post prevailing wage rate schedules at the construction site and shall update such postings as revised schedules are issued.

[Drafting Note — Cross-document conflict / open item (prevailing wage scope): The Term Sheet (Section 11.1) commits prevailing wage to "all construction performed on the Project." The TIF Memo (Section V) recommends that the Agreement "clearly delineate which construction components are subject to the Prevailing Wage Act by operation of statute and which are subject by contractual agreement," and notes that the City's strongest position is the broad interpretation that any project receiving TIF assistance, land write-downs, or other public funds triggers prevailing wage for the entire development, while Developer's counsel may resist coverage for purely private components (market-rate residential, private office, hotel). Because the Term Sheet already commits to "all construction," the broad scope is likely binding as a contractual matter. Developer should confirm the cost impact and consider whether any purely private components can be carved out; the City is likely to insist on the broad scope. Negotiate.]

**8.2 LEED Certification.** All buildings within the Project shall be designed and constructed to achieve LEED Gold certification (or an equivalent green building certification as may be approved by the City in writing) as administered by the U.S. Green Building Council. Developer shall provide evidence of LEED registration for each building prior to commencement of construction of the applicable building. The specific LEED rating system applicable to each building component shall be identified in the Definitive Plans. Developer shall submit documentation of LEED certification status to the City upon receipt of certification or, in the event certification has not been achieved, a report detailing the status of the certification process and any deficiencies. Failure to achieve LEED Gold certification for a building, despite good-faith efforts, shall not constitute a default under this Agreement so long as Developer has achieved LEED Silver certification or an equivalent approved by the City.

[Drafting Note — Developer-favorable (LEED cure): The Term Sheet (Section 11.2) requires LEED Gold but does not address the consequences of failure to achieve certification. The good-faith-efforts / LEED-Silver-fallback provision above is Developer-favorable, recognizing that LEED certification outcomes are not entirely within Developer's control. The City may seek a stricter standard or liquidated damages for failure to achieve Gold. Negotiate.]

**8.3 Insurance.** Developer shall maintain, or cause its general contractor to maintain, throughout the construction period for each phase, the following minimum insurance coverages:

| Coverage | Minimum Amount |
|---|---|
| Commercial General Liability (CGL) | $10,000,000 per occurrence / $25,000,000 in the aggregate |
| Umbrella / Excess Liability | $25,000,000 per occurrence and in the aggregate |
| Builder's Risk | Full replacement value of each phase under construction (all-risk) |
| Workers' Compensation | Statutory limits as required by Illinois law |
| Automobile Liability | $5,000,000 combined single limit |
| Professional Liability (Architect/Engineer) | $5,000,000 |
| Pollution Legal Liability | $10,000,000 |

The City of Lakemont and the Construction Lender shall be named as additional insureds on the CGL and umbrella/excess liability policies, and the Construction Lender shall be named as loss payee on the Builder's Risk and Pollution Legal Liability policies, as applicable. All carriers shall be rated A- VII or better by A.M. Best or an equivalent nationally recognized rating organization. Certificates of insurance evidencing the required coverages shall be provided to the City and the Construction Lender prior to Closing and shall be updated annually and upon renewal. All policies shall provide for at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice to the City and the Construction Lender of cancellation, non-renewal, or material modification.

[Drafting Note — Cross-document conflict (insurance limits): The Term Sheet (Section 11.3) specifies CGL of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $20,000,000 in the aggregate, and does not require Professional Liability or Pollution Legal Liability coverage. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 5) specifies CGL of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $25,000,000 in the aggregate, and requires Professional Liability of $5,000,000 and Pollution Legal Liability of $10,000,000. This Agreement adopts the higher CGL aggregate ($25,000,000) and the additional coverages required by the Construction Lender, which also benefit the City. Confirm the City concurs with the higher limits and additional coverages, and confirm the cost impact is acceptable to Developer.]

## ARTICLE 9. COMMUNITY BENEFITS

**9.1 Local Hiring.** Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to achieve a goal of 35% Lakemont residents for construction workforce employment across all phases of the Project. This goal shall be measured on a cumulative project-wide basis and shall encompass direct employees and employees of general contractors and subcontractors performing construction work on the Site. Developer shall submit quarterly reports to the City's Community Development Department documenting local hiring percentages, including workforce data by trade category, residency information, and total labor hours, within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter during which active construction is occurring. Failure to achieve the 35% local hiring goal in any given quarter or on a cumulative basis shall not, in and of itself, constitute a default under this Agreement, provided that Developer demonstrates good-faith efforts to achieve the goal, including at minimum posting job openings with the City's workforce development partners, attending local job fairs, and providing reasonable notice of hiring opportunities to the Community Development Department.

[Drafting Note — Cross-document conflict (local hiring measurement and enforceability): The Resolution (Condition 3(c)(i)) requires that "not less than thirty-five percent (35%) of all construction labor hours on the Project be performed by individuals who are residents of the City of Lakemont," measured by labor hours. The Term Sheet (Section 9) frames the 35% as a "goal" measured on a "cumulative project-wide basis" encompassing "direct employees and employees of general contractors, subcontractors, and material suppliers," with failure not constituting a default upon a showing of good-faith efforts. There are three conflicts: (i) measurement basis—labor hours (Resolution) versus employment/headcount (Term Sheet); (ii) scope—the Term Sheet includes material suppliers, which the Resolution's "construction labor hours" may not; and (iii) enforceability—the Resolution's "not less than 35%" reads as a firmer requirement, while the Term Sheet's "goal" with good-faith compliance is softer. This Agreement adopts the Term Sheet's goal-and-good-faith framing (Developer-favorable) but measures by labor hours (consistent with the Resolution) and excludes material suppliers from the calculation (Developer-favorable, since material suppliers are off-site and not reasonably controllable). The City is likely to insist on the labor-hours measure and may resist the exclusion of material suppliers and the good-faith-only framing. Negotiate.]

**9.2 Living Wage.** Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to require that all permanent employees employed at retail establishments and hotel operations within the Project be compensated at a wage rate of not less than $17.50 per hour, indexed annually thereafter by the greater of the Consumer Price Index or 3%. Developer shall include a living wage covenant in leases for retail space and in the hotel operating agreement, to the extent commercially reasonable and enforceable against the applicable tenant or operator. Developer's obligations under this Section 9.2 are obligations of commercially reasonable effort and shall not constitute a default if a tenant or operator fails to comply notwithstanding Developer's good-faith efforts to impose and enforce the covenant.

[Drafting Note — Cross-document conflict (living wage — new obligation from Resolution): The Resolution (Condition 3(c)(ii)) mandates a living wage of not less than $17.50 per hour for "all permanent employees employed at retail establishments and hotel operations within the Project." The Term Sheet (Section 9) does not mention any living wage requirement, and the RFP Response does not mention a living wage. This is a new obligation introduced by the Resolution that is not reflected in the Term Sheet. This Agreement incorporates the living wage but (i) limits it to permanent retail and hotel employees (not construction, which is covered by prevailing wage, and not office or property-management employees, which the Resolution does not reach), (ii) frames it as a commercially reasonable effort enforceable through lease/operator covenants (Developer-favorable, recognizing Developer does not directly employ retail/hotel workers), (iii) adds CPI indexing (which the Resolution does not specify—confirm whether the City requires indexing), and (iv) provides that tenant/operator non-compliance is not a Developer default upon good-faith efforts. The City is likely to seek a harder obligation and broader coverage. Negotiate scope, indexing, and the default consequences.]

**9.3 First-Source Hiring.** Developer and its commercial tenants shall partner with the Lakemont Workforce Center for the recruitment, training, and hiring of individuals to fill permanent employment positions created within the Project, by notifying the Lakemont Workforce Center of available permanent positions prior to broader recruitment. Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to implement a first-source hiring program, and shall include a first-source hiring covenant in leases for retail and office space and in the hotel operating agreement, to the extent commercially reasonable. Developer's obligations under this Section 9.3 are obligations of commercially reasonable effort and shall not constitute a default if a tenant or operator does not hire through the Lakemont Workforce Center notwithstanding Developer's good-faith efforts.

[Drafting Note — Cross-document conflict (first-source hiring for permanent positions): The Resolution (Condition 3(c)(iii)) requires a first-source hiring program under which Developer and its commercial tenants partner with the Lakemont Workforce Center for permanent employment positions. The Term Sheet (Section 9) is silent on first-source hiring and on permanent positions (it addresses only construction local hiring). The RFP Response (Section 6.2) describes a first-source hiring program with the Lakemont Workforce Center for both construction and permanent positions. This Agreement incorporates the first-source program for permanent positions as a commercially reasonable effort (Developer-favorable), consistent with the RFP Response. The City is likely to seek a firmer obligation. Negotiate.]

**9.4 Additional Community Benefits.** Any additional community benefit obligations beyond those set forth in this Article 9 shall be as set forth in the Community Benefits provisions of this Agreement and Exhibit F. The Parties acknowledge that the Resolution (Condition 3(c)) requires the Agreement to incorporate a Community Benefits Agreement or Community Benefits provisions; the provisions of this Article 9 and Exhibit F are intended to satisfy that requirement.

[Drafting Note — Open item (Community Benefits Agreement form): The Resolution (Condition 3(c)) requires the Agreement to "incorporate a Community Benefits Agreement (CBA) or Community Benefits provisions." The Term Sheet (Section 9) states that "additional community benefit obligations, if any, shall be set forth in the definitive Agreement." Confirm whether the City requires a standalone CBA (potentially with third-party community signatories) or whether the community benefits provisions embedded in this Agreement and Exhibit F are sufficient. A standalone CBA with third-party signatories could create additional enforcement and compliance risks for Developer; Developer should prefer embedded provisions.]

## ARTICLE 10. AFFORDABLE HOUSING

**10.1 Affordable Units.** Developer shall include at least 106 Affordable Units across the Project (64 in Phase I and 42 in Phase III), representing 20% of the 530 total residential units, with rents restricted to households earning at or below 60% of AMI.

**10.2 Distribution and Quality.** Affordable Units shall be distributed proportionally across unit types (studio, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom) and shall not be segregated or concentrated on particular floors or in separate wings of the buildings. Affordable Units shall be comparable in design, finishes, and amenity access to market-rate units within the same building, subject to reasonable variations consistent with industry practice. Residents of Affordable Units shall have full access to all building amenities.

**10.3 Affordability Restriction Period.** Affordability restrictions shall remain in effect for a minimum of thirty (30) years from the date of issuance of the CO for the applicable phase. The affordability restrictions shall be memorialized by a restrictive covenant recorded against the applicable parcel and running with the land, in a form to be set forth in Exhibit G and acceptable to the City and the Construction Lender.

**10.4 Compliance and Reporting.** Developer shall comply with the City's Affordable Housing Compliance Program and shall provide annual income certification documentation to the City's Community Development Department for all Affordable Units throughout the affordability restriction period.

[Drafting Note — Open item (affordable housing restrictive covenant form): The Term Sheet (Section 12) provides that the affordability restrictions shall be memorialized by a recorded restrictive covenant "in a form to be set forth in the definitive Agreement." The form of restrictive covenant (Exhibit G) must be drafted and approved by the City and the Construction Lender (the Construction Lender will require the covenant to be a Permitted Exception and may require subordination to its mortgage). The 30-year restriction period is a long-term encumbrance; confirm the Construction Lender's subordination requirements and the impact on permanent financing/take-out. Negotiate the form of covenant.]

## ARTICLE 11. DEVELOPMENT SCHEDULE AND MILESTONES

**11.1 Phasing Schedule.** The Project shall be constructed in four phases in accordance with the following schedule:

| Phase | Commencement Deadline | Substantial Completion Deadline |
|---|---|---|
| Phase I | Groundbreaking no later than March 1, 2026 | September 30, 2028 |
| Phase II | Within 12 months of Phase I Substantial Completion | Within 24 months of Phase II commencement |
| Phase III | Within 12 months of Phase II Substantial Completion | Within 30 months of Phase III commencement |
| Phase IV | Concurrent with Phase III commencement | Within 24 months of Phase IV commencement |

**11.2 Commencement Measurement.** The commencement deadline for each subsequent phase is measured from the Substantial Completion date of the immediately preceding phase, as may be extended by applicable Force Majeure events under Article 12. Failure to commence any phase within the specified deadline, as may be extended, shall constitute a material default under this Agreement, subject to the default and cure provisions of Article 13.

**11.3 Substantial Completion.** "Substantial Completion" means, with respect to a phase, the completion of construction of the applicable phase to the point where the building or improvements can be occupied and used for their intended purpose, as evidenced by the issuance of a temporary or final certificate of occupancy by the City's Building Department. For the avoidance of doubt, issuance of a temporary certificate of occupancy shall constitute Substantial Completion.

[Drafting Note — Developer-favorable (Substantial Completion definition): The Term Sheet (Section 13) defines Substantial Completion by reference to issuance of "a temporary or final certificate of occupancy." This Agreement expressly provides that a temporary CO constitutes Substantial Completion (Developer-favorable, earlier release of performance security and earlier commencement of the next phase). The Resolution (Condition 3(h)) references Substantial Completion "as such term shall be defined in the Development Agreement." The City may seek to require a final CO for Substantial Completion, which would delay the release of the Performance Security and the next-phase commencement clock. Negotiate.]

**11.4 Construction Progress Reports.** Developer shall provide quarterly construction progress reports to the City's Community Development Department, including percentage of completion, updated schedule, and identification of any delays or anticipated deviations from the approved timeline, within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter.

[Drafting Note — Cross-document conflict (Phase I commencement triggers): The Term Sheet contains an internal inconsistency regarding Phase I commencement. Section 13 sets the Phase I groundbreaking deadline at "no later than March 1, 2026." Section 15(c)(b) provides that the City may terminate if "Developer's failure to commence Phase I construction within 6 months following land conveyance." If Closing occurs in mid-2025 (the target), the 6-month commencement trigger would occur in late 2025—approximately three months before the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking deadline—meaning the City's termination right could be triggered even if Developer is on schedule under Section 13. This Agreement resolves the inconsistency by adopting the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking deadline as the controlling Phase I commencement milestone and omitting the 6-month-from-conveyance trigger. Confirm the City concurs, and reconcile the Resolution's 36-month-from-conveyance reversionary trigger (see Article 14 drafting note) with the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking and September 30, 2028 Substantial Completion deadlines.]

## ARTICLE 12. FORCE MAJEURE

**12.1 Definition.** "Force Majeure" means any event beyond the reasonable control of Developer that prevents or materially delays performance, including, without limitation: acts of God, fire, flood, earthquake, hurricane, tornado, severe weather events, pandemic, epidemic, quarantine, governmental health orders, war, terrorism, civil unrest, insurrection, strikes or labor disputes (other than those caused by Developer's labor practices or Developer's failure to comply with applicable labor laws), industry-wide supply chain disruptions beyond Developer's reasonable control, governmental moratoria on construction, and actions or inactions of governmental authorities (other than the City of Lakemont) that prevent or materially delay construction.

**12.2 Effect.** Force Majeure events shall extend the applicable deadlines for commencement and Substantial Completion on a day-for-day basis for the duration of the Force Majeure event.

**12.3 Aggregate Cap.** The maximum extension of time available under this Article 12 shall not exceed eighteen (18) months in the aggregate across the Project.

**12.4 Notice.** Developer shall provide written notice to the City within fifteen (15) business days of the occurrence of a Force Majeure event, describing in reasonable detail the nature of the event, its anticipated duration, and its expected impact on the Project timeline, with updated notices as circumstances change.

**12.5 Mitigation.** Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to mitigate the effects of any Force Majeure event, including pursuing alternative suppliers, adjusting construction sequencing, and engaging additional contractors as reasonably available.

**12.6 Exclusions.** Force Majeure shall not include: financial inability to perform, changes in market conditions, increases in the cost of labor or materials (unless attributable to an industry-wide supply chain disruption), Developer's failure to obtain financing, or any event caused by Developer's negligence or willful misconduct.

[Drafting Note — Cross-document conflict (force majeure cap): The Term Sheet (Section 14) provides an 18-month aggregate Force Majeure cap across the Project. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 9, Event of Default 9) provides that failure to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion by September 30, 2028 is "subject to force majeure extensions of up to 180 days" (6 months) plus Lender-approved extension periods under the Loan Term Sheet's extension options. The Construction Lender's 6-month Phase I force majeure allowance is significantly shorter than the 18-month aggregate cap in this Agreement. The Loan Documents may impose a tighter Phase I-specific cap that could trigger a loan default before this Agreement's deadlines are breached. Developer should negotiate consistency between the Agreement's 18-month aggregate cap and the Loan Documents' force majeure and extension provisions, and should seek Lender approval of the 18-month cap (or a mutually acceptable Phase I-specific sub-cap). Negotiate with the Construction Lender.]

## ARTICLE 13. DEFAULTS AND REMEDIES

**13.1 Monetary Defaults.** In the event of a monetary default by either Party (including Developer's failure to pay the Purchase Price or the City's failure to fund committed infrastructure amounts), the non-defaulting Party shall provide written notice specifying the nature and amount of the default. The defaulting Party shall have sixty (60) days from receipt of such notice to cure the default.

**13.2 Non-Monetary Defaults.** In the event of a non-monetary default by either Party (including failure to commence construction within applicable deadlines, failure to comply with affordable housing requirements, or failure to maintain required insurance), the non-defaulting Party shall provide written notice specifying the nature of the default in reasonable detail. The defaulting Party shall have ninety (90) days from receipt of such notice to cure the default; provided, however, that if the nature of the default is such that it cannot reasonably be cured within 90 days, the defaulting Party shall have such additional time as is reasonably necessary to effect a cure, so long as the defaulting Party has commenced cure efforts within the initial 90-day period and is diligently and continuously pursuing cure to completion.

**13.3 Remedies.** Upon a material breach by a Party that remains uncured after the expiration of the applicable cure period (as such cure period may be extended for the Construction Lender under Section 17.2), the non-defaulting Party shall have all remedies available at law or in equity, including damages, specific performance, and injunctive relief, subject in all cases to the dispute resolution provisions of Article 18. The Parties acknowledge that the remedies available to the City shall be subject to the limitations set forth in Article 14 (including the absence of any reversionary interest in the Site).

[Drafting Note — Developer-favorable (cure periods): The 60-day monetary and 90-day non-monetary cure periods (with diligent-pursuit extension) are drawn from the Term Sheet (Section 15) and are Developer-favorable. The Construction Lender requires additional cure periods beyond Developer's (Section 17.2), which this Agreement grants; those Lender cure rights also protect Developer by preventing a City termination that would trigger a loan default. The City may seek shorter cure periods; negotiate.]

## ARTICLE 14. TERMINATION

**14.1 Termination by City.** The City shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon: (a) a material breach by Developer that remains uncured after the expiration of the applicable cure period (as extended for the Construction Lender under Section 17.2); (b) Developer's abandonment of the Project, evidenced by cessation of all construction activity for a period of 180 consecutive days (other than cessation caused by Force Majeure events); and (c) such other events of default as may be specified in this Agreement. The City's remedies upon termination shall be limited to those set forth in Section 13.3 and shall not include any reversionary interest in, or right of re-entry with respect to, the Site.

**14.2 Termination by Developer.** Developer shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon: (a) the City's failure to deliver marketable and insurable fee simple title to the Site at Closing in accordance with Section 3.3; (b) the City's failure to fund or complete its committed off-site infrastructure obligations ($12,500,000) within the timelines specified in Exhibit D; (c) a condemnation or taking by eminent domain by any entity other than the City of more than 15% of the Site; (d) the imposition, creation, or recording of any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, or defeasible condition in favor of the City or any other party with respect to the Site, other than as expressly permitted under this Agreement; (e) the City's material breach that remains uncured after the expiration of the applicable cure period; and (f) such other events of default as may be specified in this Agreement.

**14.3 No Reversionary Interest; Alternative Completion Assurance.** The Parties expressly acknowledge and agree that the City shall not hold, and the Site shall not be subject to, any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, fee simple determinable, fee simple subject to condition subsequent, or any other form of defeasible fee estate. Developer's fee simple title to the Site shall be absolute and indefeasible, subject only to the Permitted Exceptions. In lieu of any reversionary interest, the Parties adopt the following alternative completion assurance mechanism to address the City's interest in timely Phase I completion:

(a) **Contractual Repurchase Option.** In the event Developer fails to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion within the period equal to (i) the Phase I Substantial Completion deadline (September 30, 2028), plus (ii) all applicable cure periods under Article 13, plus (iii) all applicable Force Majeure extensions under Article 12, plus (iv) the Construction Lender's additional cure period under Section 17.2 (the "Completion Outside Date"), the City shall have a contractual option to repurchase the Site from Developer, exercisable only upon (A) the Completion Outside Date having occurred, (B) the City's delivery of written notice of exercise within ninety (90) days after the Completion Outside Date, and (C) the Construction Lender's prior written consent or the full satisfaction of the outstanding Construction Loan balance. The repurchase price shall be the lesser of (x) the Purchase Price paid by Developer or (y) the then-current fair market value of the Site as determined by an independent MAI appraiser mutually selected by the Parties.

(b) **Liquidated Damages Alternative.** As an alternative to the repurchase option, the Parties may agree, at the City's election, that Developer shall pay liquidated damages to the City in a predetermined amount representing a reasonable pre-estimate of the City's actual damages resulting from the completion failure, which liquidated damages shall constitute an unsecured contractual obligation of Developer and not a lien on the Site.

(c) **Enhanced Completion Guaranty.** Developer shall provide, or cause to be provided, an enhanced completion guaranty from Marcus J. Pellegrini and/or the Pellegrini Family Trust guaranteeing completion of Phase I within the agreed timeframe, providing the City with a direct contractual remedy against the guarantors (and their assets other than the Site) in the event of a completion failure, in form and substance reasonably acceptable to the City and the Construction Lender.

[Drafting Note — CRITICAL cross-document conflict (reversionary interest / clawback): This is the most significant cross-document conflict in the transaction. (1) The Resolution (Condition 3(h)) requires Developer to grant the City a "reversionary interest" in the Site such that, if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within 36 months of the date of conveyance, fee simple title to the Site (together with all improvements) shall automatically revert to the City, subject to the City refunding the Purchase Price (net of damages). (2) The Loan Term Sheet (Sections 4.2, 7.1, 9, and 12) STRICTLY PROHIBITS any reversionary interest, defeasible fee, right of re-entry, or possibility of reverter, and states that any such interest would render the title uninsurable, subordinate the Construction Lender's mortgage to the City's right to reclaim the property, prevent foreclosure, and render the Construction Loan "impossible to close." The Construction Lender expressly requires the Development Agreement to eliminate the reversionary interest and substitute an alternative clawback mechanism. (3) The Term Sheet does not mention any reversionary interest. (4) The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 7(d)) requires Investor consent for any clawback, reversionary interest, right of re-entry, repurchase option, or similar mechanism, and acknowledges that the Resolution contemplates such a mechanism and that the parties must negotiate terms compatible with the Construction Lender's requirements and Investor's interests. This Agreement eliminates the reversionary interest (Developer-favorable and required by the Construction Lender) and substitutes a contractual repurchase option, liquidated damages alternative, and enhanced completion guaranty, drawn from the Construction Lender's suggested alternatives. The City must be persuaded to accept the substitution; the Resolution's reversionary interest cannot be incorporated as written without defeating the Construction Loan. This is the central negotiation issue and must be resolved before the Construction Lender will approve the Agreement.]

[Drafting Note — Cross-document conflict (36-month reversionary trigger vs. Phase I timeline): Even setting aside the title-insurability problem, the Resolution's 36-month-from-conveyance reversionary trigger is inconsistent with the Project schedule. If Closing occurs in mid-2025 (the target), the 36-month reversionary deadline would occur in mid-2028—approximately three months before the contractual Phase I Substantial Completion deadline of September 30, 2028. Accordingly, the reversionary interest would be triggered even if Developer is performing on schedule under the Term Sheet. Moreover, the 36-month period runs from conveyance, but the Phase I groundbreaking deadline is March 1, 2026 (up to approximately nine months after a mid-2025 conveyance), leaving only approximately 27 months for a 30-month construction period. The alternative completion assurance mechanism in Section 14.3 ties the City's remedy to the contractual Phase I deadlines (as extended by cure periods, Force Majeure, and the Construction Lender's cure period), which is consistent with the Project schedule. Confirm the City accepts this alignment.]

## ARTICLE 15. INDEMNIFICATION

**15.1 Mutual Indemnification.** Each Party shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the other Party, and its officers, employees, agents, and representatives, from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from or related to the indemnifying Party's negligent acts or omissions, reckless conduct, or willful misconduct in connection with the performance of its obligations under this Agreement.

**15.2 Developer Indemnification of City.** Developer shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the City from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to construction activities on the Site, including without limitation claims for personal injury, death, property damage, and workers' compensation, regardless of whether such claims are brought by Developer's employees, contractors, subcontractors, or third parties.

**15.3 City Indemnification of Developer.** The City shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Developer, its members, officers, employees, agents, contractors, and successors, from and against all claims, demands, actions, losses, costs, damages, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to pre-existing environmental conditions on the City-owned parcels, as more fully provided in Section 5.4.

**15.4 Survival.** The indemnification obligations set forth in this Article 15 shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement.

## ARTICLE 16. ASSIGNMENT AND CHANGE OF CONTROL

**16.1 Restriction on Assignment.** Developer may not assign this Agreement or any interest therein, in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of the City, which consent may be withheld in the City's reasonable discretion.

**16.2 Permitted Assignments.** Notwithstanding Section 16.1, Developer may assign this Agreement without the City's prior written consent to:

(a) an entity in which Marcus J. Pellegrini (or the Pellegrini Family Trust) holds at least a 50% controlling interest and which entity expressly assumes in writing all of Developer's obligations under this Agreement;

(b) an entity controlled by Marcus J. Pellegrini or the Pellegrini Family Trust for estate-planning purposes, provided Marcus J. Pellegrini retains management control of Developer or the assignee;

(c) a transferee that is an existing member of Developer (Pellegrini Family Trust, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, or TransitWorks Equity Group LLC), or an affiliate of an existing member, provided that the transfer does not reduce the Pellegrini Interest below 50% and the transferee assumes in writing all of Developer's obligations under this Agreement;

(d) a transferee in connection with a capital raise that has been approved by Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP (to the extent required under the Lakeshore Side Letter) and by the City, provided that the transferee assumes in writing all of Developer's obligations under this Agreement; and

(e) a Construction Lender-designee or a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale (or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure) under the Loan Documents, without the City's consent, or if City consent is required, such consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed, and the City shall respond within thirty (30) days of request.

Developer shall provide the City with written notice of any permitted assignment, together with a copy of the assignment and assumption agreement, within fifteen (15) days of the effective date of such assignment.

**16.3 Key Person Provision.** The Parties acknowledge that the City's selection of Developer was based, in substantial part, on the qualifications, experience, and financial capacity of Marcus J. Pellegrini. Marcus J. Pellegrini shall serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of Developer and shall exercise day-to-day management authority over the Project. If Marcus J. Pellegrini ceases to serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of Developer, Developer shall, within sixty (60) days, appoint a replacement reasonably acceptable to the City; provided, however, that no such replacement shall be required to be approved by the City if the Pellegrini Interest remains at or above 50% and the replacement is an experienced real estate development executive. The Key Person provision in this Section 16.3 is intended as an alternative and supplement to the percentage-of-interest thresholds in Section 16.2, to accommodate internal transfers and capital raises that do not diminish Pellegrini's active management of the Project.

**16.4 Cure Period.** No purported assignment in violation of this Article 16 shall constitute a default until Developer has failed to cure such violation within sixty (60) days following written notice from the City. Any purported assignment in violation of this Article 16 that remains uncured after the 60-day cure period shall be void and of no force and effect and shall constitute a material default under this Agreement.

**16.5 Definition of Assignment.** For purposes of this Article 16, "assignment" shall include any direct or indirect transfer, conveyance, pledge, hypothecation, or other disposition of this Agreement or any interest therein, whether voluntary or involuntary, by operation of law or otherwise; provided, that pledges of membership interests to the Construction Lender as collateral under the Loan Documents shall not constitute an assignment for purposes of this Article 16.

[Drafting Note — Cross-document conflict (assignment / change-of-control thresholds): Three different Pellegrini-interest thresholds appear across the documents: (i) the Term Sheet (Section 10) requires Pellegrini (or the Pellegrini Family Trust) to hold "at least a 50% controlling interest" for a permitted assignment; (ii) the Loan Term Sheet (Section 8.1) treats a Change of Control as occurring if Pellegrini ceases to hold "at least a [50]% direct or indirect ownership interest," with the [50]% threshold expressly "subject to further discussion and negotiation"; and (iii) the Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 5.2) treats a Change of Control as occurring if the "Pellegrini Interest falls below fifty-one percent (51%)" and grants Lakeshore enhanced consent rights if the Pellegrini Interest falls below 51%. The Side Letter (Section 8.3) expressly requires the Agreement's assignment and change-of-control provisions to be harmonized with (a) Lakeshore's anti-dilution, consent, and governance rights, (b) anticipated future capital raises, and (c) the Construction Lender's assignment, change-of-control, and equity-maintenance covenants, and to include carve-outs for internal transfers, capital raises approved by both Investor and the City, a Key Person provision, and a cure period of not less than 60 days. This Agreement adopts the 50% threshold (consistent with the Term Sheet and the Loan Term Sheet's bracketed figure), adds the carve-outs and Key Person provision required by the Side Letter, and adds the 60-day cure period. The 51% threshold in the Side Letter is an internal matter between Developer and Lakeshore and does not need to be replicated in this Agreement, but Developer must ensure that any capital raise that dilutes the Pellegrini Interest below 51% (which would trigger Lakeshore's Change of Control rights under the Side Letter) is coordinated with Lakeshore. Confirm the Construction Lender's final [50]% threshold and ensure consistency. Negotiate with the City, the Construction Lender, and Lakeshore.]

[Drafting Note — Open item (Construction Lender foreclosure assignment): The Loan Term Sheet (Section 12(e)) requires the Agreement to permit assignment to a Construction Lender-designee or a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale (or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu) without the City's consent, or with consent not unreasonably withheld and a 30-day response. Section 16.2(e) above incorporates this requirement. Confirm the City accepts assignment to a foreclosure purchaser without consent (the City may seek a right of first refusal or consent rights). Negotiate.]

## ARTICLE 17. LENDER PROTECTIONS

**17.1 Lender Notice.** The City shall provide the Construction Lender with prompt written notice of any default or alleged default by Developer under this Agreement, concurrently with any notice delivered to Developer.

**17.2 Lender Cure Rights.** The Construction Lender shall have the right (but not the obligation) to cure any default by Developer under this Agreement. The Construction Lender's additional cure period shall be not less than thirty (30) days beyond Developer's cure period for monetary defaults and not less than sixty (60) days beyond Developer's cure period for non-monetary defaults, with such periods extended as necessary if the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure that requires additional time (including time necessary to obtain possession of the Site through foreclosure or appointment of a receiver). The City shall not terminate this Agreement or exercise any remedy on account of a Developer default during the Construction Lender's cure period.

**17.3 Lender Consent to Amendments.** No material amendment, modification, supplement, or termination of this Agreement shall be effective without the Construction Lender's prior written consent, which shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed.

**17.4 Lender Review and Approval.** This Agreement and all Exhibits, Schedules, and ancillary documents shall be delivered to the Construction Lender in draft form not less than fifteen (15) business days prior to the proposed execution date and shall not be executed until the Construction Lender has confirmed in writing that they are in form and substance acceptable to it. The Construction Lender's review shall not be unreasonably delayed. The Permitted Exceptions (Exhibit B) and any institutional controls or ELUCs shall be subject to the Construction Lender's review and approval.

**17.5 Subordination of City Interests.** Any lien, encumbrance, or security interest that the City may hold or acquire in the Site (other than the City's rights under the alternative completion assurance mechanism in Section 14.3, which are contractual and not real-property interests) shall be subordinated to the Construction Lender's mortgage pursuant to a subordination and intercreditor agreement in form and substance acceptable to the Construction Lender.

**17.6 Foreclosure; Deed-in-Lieu.** This Agreement shall be assignable to a Construction Lender-designee or a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale (or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure) as provided in Section 16.2(e).

**17.7 Estoppel Certificates.** The City shall deliver estoppel certificates to the Construction Lender upon reasonable request (not more than twice per calendar year), certifying the status of this Agreement, the existence or absence of any outstanding defaults, and such other customary matters as the Construction Lender may reasonably request, within thirty (30) days of request.

**17.8 TIF Assignment.** Developer may assign its rights to receive TIF Reimbursement to the Construction Lender as collateral under the Loan Documents, and the City shall cooperate with such assignment, including by making TIF Reimbursement payments in accordance with the Construction Lender's instructions to the extent permitted by applicable law; provided, that the City's obligation to make TIF Reimbursement payments remains subject to the availability of increment as provided in Section 4.2(c).

[Drafting Note — Developer-favorable / lender requirement (Lender Protections): The Lender Protections in this Article 17 are drawn from the Loan Term Sheet (Section 12) and are essential to the Construction Loan closing. While these provisions protect the Construction Lender, they also protect Developer by (i) preventing a City termination that would trigger a loan default and cross-default, (ii) preserving the Construction Lender's security and remedies, and (iii) ensuring the Agreement is in a form acceptable to the lender. The City is likely to resist some of these provisions (particularly the Lender's extended cure periods, the consent-to-amendments right, and the foreclosure-assignment right), but they are conditions to the Construction Loan. The TIF assignment in Section 17.8 must be reconciled with the City's "subject to availability" position (Section 4.2(c)) and the TIF Memo's recommendation that TIF shortfall not be a City default. Negotiate with the City and the Construction Lender.]

[Drafting Note — Open item (Lakeshore consent rights): The Lakeshore Side Letter (Sections 4 and 7) grants Lakeshore (i) the right to review and approve the final form of this Agreement at least 15 business days before execution, (ii) consent rights over Material Amendments (including changes to the TIF cap, abatement schedule, phasing timelines beyond 2035, environmental obligations exceeding $3,140,000, performance security, and assignment/change-of-control provisions), (iii) enhanced consent rights if the Pellegrini Interest falls below 51%, and (iv) restrictions on Developer incurring Project debt above $268,400,000, granting liens, modifying the phasing schedule, agreeing to any clawback/reversionary interest, or waiving material Developer rights. These consent rights are internal to Developer and Lakeshore and are not City obligations, but they constrain Developer's negotiation flexibility and must be satisfied before Developer can execute or amend this Agreement. Developer must obtain Lakeshore's approval of this Agreement and any Material Amendment. Coordinate.]

## ARTICLE 18. DISPUTE RESOLUTION

**18.1 Negotiation.** The Parties shall first attempt to resolve any dispute arising under or related to this Agreement through good-faith negotiation between the City Manager (or designee) and Developer's CEO (or designee) for a period of thirty (30) days following written notice of the dispute by either Party.

**18.2 Mediation.** If the dispute is not resolved through negotiation within the 30-day period, the dispute shall be submitted to non-binding mediation before the Lakemont Mediation Center, or such other neutral mediator as the Parties may agree upon. The costs of mediation, including mediator fees, shall be shared equally by the Parties. Each Party shall bear its own attorneys' fees in connection with the mediation.

**18.3 Binding Arbitration.** If the dispute is not resolved through mediation within sixty (60) days following submission to the mediator, the dispute shall be submitted to final and binding arbitration administered under the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association, conducted by a single arbitrator with experience in real estate development matters.

**18.4 Venue; Governing Law.** All mediation and arbitration proceedings shall be conducted in Cook County or Lake County, Illinois. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Illinois, without regard to conflict-of-laws principles.

**18.5 Provisional Remedies.** Nothing in this Article 18 shall prevent either Party from seeking temporary restraining orders, preliminary injunctions, or other provisional judicial relief where necessary to prevent irreparable harm, pending resolution of the dispute through the procedures set forth above.

[Drafting Note — Open item (dispute resolution venue and county): The Term Sheet (Section 17) provides for venue in "Cook County or Lake County, Illinois" and Illinois governing law. The Loan Term Sheet (Section 13) provides for litigation venue in Cook County and Illinois governing law. The county conflict noted in the Section 3.1 drafting note (Cook County PINs per the Phase II ESA vs. Lake County Recorder per the RFP Response) also bears on venue. Confirm the correct county for the Site and align the dispute-resolution venue accordingly.]

## ARTICLE 19. CONDITIONS PRECEDENT

**19.1 Conditions Precedent to Execution of this Agreement.** The execution of this Agreement shall be subject to the satisfaction or waiver of the following conditions precedent:

(a) Rezoning of the Site from M-2 to PD-TOD approved by the City Council and effective under applicable law;

(b) Parcel consolidation of Parcels 015, 016, and 017 completed or authorized by the applicable county authority;

(c) IEPA approval of the RAP for Parcel 016 under the VSRP;

(d) Developer's demonstration of committed equity of at least $85,000,000, in form and substance reasonably acceptable to the City, verified by Clearmont Accounting Group LLP;

(e) Approval of this Agreement by the Lakemont City Council at a duly noticed public meeting; and

(f) Confirmation from First Prairie National Bank that this Agreement is in a form acceptable to the lender as a condition of the Construction Loan commitment.

**19.2 Conditions Precedent to Closing.** The Closing of the conveyance of the Site shall be subject to the satisfaction or waiver of the following conditions precedent:

(a) Execution of this Agreement by both Parties and approval by the City Council;

(b) Completion of rezoning of the Site from M-2 to PD-TOD;

(c) IEPA approval of the RAP for Parcel 016 (and, if a phased conveyance is adopted under Section 3.4, issuance of the NFR Letter for Parcel 016 prior to conveyance of Parcel 016);

(d) Completion or authorization of parcel consolidation;

(e) Developer's demonstration of committed equity of at least $85,000,000;

(f) Developer's posting of the $15,000,000 Performance Security; and

(g) Satisfaction of all conditions precedent to the Construction Loan closing, including issuance of the ALTA lender's title insurance policy and delivery of all insurance certificates.

[Drafting Note — Open item (City Council approval of the executed Agreement): The Term Sheet (Section 18.2(v)) lists City Council approval of the definitive Agreement as a condition precedent to execution. The Resolution authorizes the City Manager to negotiate and execute the Agreement subject to the Resolution's conditions and approval as to form by Corporation Counsel and outside counsel, but it is not entirely clear whether separate City Council approval of the executed Agreement is required in addition to the Resolution's conditional approval. Confirm whether the executed Agreement must be presented to the City Council for a separate approval vote, and build the appropriate lead time into the schedule.]

[Drafting Note — Open item (Construction Lender conditions): The Loan Term Sheet (Section 7.1) lists 17 conditions precedent to the Construction Loan closing, including a GMP/stipulated-sum construction contract, architect's certificate, legal opinions, guaranty agreements, and an intercreditor/subordination agreement. Several of these (e.g., the construction contract and architect's certificate) are not within the City's control but must be satisfied before the Construction Loan funds and before Closing can occur in a manner acceptable to the Construction Lender. Coordinate the Closing conditions with the Construction Loan closing conditions.]

## ARTICLE 20. MISCELLANEOUS

**20.1 Representations and Warranties.** Each Party represents and warrants that it has full power and authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform its obligations hereunder, and that the execution and delivery of this Agreement has been duly authorized by all necessary action. Developer represents and warrants that it has the financial capacity, development experience, and organizational resources necessary to complete the Project. The City represents and warrants that it has good and marketable fee simple title to the Site, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, and adverse claims (except Permitted Exceptions), and that it has the legal authority to convey the Site to Developer. The City represents and warrants that the Site is located within the TIF District and that TIF funds are legally available for the purposes described in this Agreement, subject to annual appropriation and the availability of increment.

**20.2 Notices.** All notices, demands, requests, consents, approvals, and other communications required or permitted under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed delivered upon personal delivery, upon the date of delivery by nationally recognized overnight courier, or three (3) business days following deposit in the United States mail, registered or certified, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, addressed as follows:

If to Developer:
Granite City Development LLC, 900 West Monroe Street, Suite 2200, Chicago, IL 60607, Attn: Marcus J. Pellegrini, Manager and CEO
With a copy to: Thornfield & Pratt LLP, 311 South Wacker Drive, Suite 5400, Chicago, IL 60606, Attn: Catherine M. Albrecht

If to City:
City of Lakemont, Office of the City Manager, 200 Civic Center Drive, Lakemont, IL 60048, Attn: Gerald T. Fong, City Manager
With a copy to: Office of Corporation Counsel, City of Lakemont, 200 Civic Center Drive, Lakemont, IL 60048, Attn: Rebecca A. Sinclair, Esq.
With a copy to: Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP, 150 North Michigan Avenue, Suite 3200, Chicago, IL 60601, Attn: Jonathan Ashford

Either Party may change its notice address by providing written notice to the other Party in accordance with this Section 20.2. The Construction Lender shall be entitled to receive copies of all notices of default delivered under this Agreement, as provided in Section 17.1.

**20.3 Confidentiality.** The terms and contents of this Agreement are confidential and shall not be disclosed by either Party to any third party, except: (a) to each Party's legal counsel, financial advisors, accountants, lenders, and equity investors, as reasonably necessary; (b) as required by applicable law or governmental regulation, including the Illinois Freedom of Information Act (5 ILCS 140/) as applicable to the City; and (c) to the extent necessary for City Council proceedings, committee meetings, and other governmental approvals related to the Project.

**20.4 Expenses.** Each Party shall bear its own costs and expenses incurred in connection with the negotiation and preparation of this Agreement, including legal fees, consultant fees, and other professional expenses.

**20.5 Severability.** If any provision of this Agreement is held invalid or unenforceable by a court or arbitrator of competent jurisdiction, such invalidity or unenforceability shall not affect the other provisions of this Agreement, which shall remain in full force and effect.

**20.6 Entire Agreement; Supersession.** This Agreement, together with its Exhibits and Schedules, constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes the Term Sheet in its entirety upon execution and delivery by both Parties. The Term Sheet shall be of no further force and effect upon execution of this Agreement.

**20.7 Amendments.** This Agreement may be amended, modified, or supplemented only by a written instrument executed by both Parties, and, with respect to any material amendment, by the Construction Lender (to the extent required under Section 17.3) and Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP (to the extent required under the Lakeshore Side Letter).

**20.8 Counterparts; Electronic Signatures.** This Agreement may be executed in any number of counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original and all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument. Signatures transmitted by electronic means shall be deemed original signatures for all purposes.

**20.9 Survival.** The provisions of Article 5 (Environmental Matters), Article 10 (Affordable Housing), Article 15 (Indemnification), and such other provisions as by their nature are intended to survive shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement.

**20.10 No Third-Party Beneficiaries.** Except for the Construction Lender's rights under Article 17, this Agreement is for the sole benefit of the Parties and their permitted successors and assigns, and nothing herein is intended to confer rights upon any other person.

**20.11 Construction Lender as Intended Beneficiary.** The Construction Lender is an intended beneficiary of the Lender Protections set forth in Article 17 and may enforce such provisions to the extent permitted under the Loan Documents.

## EXHIBITS

The following Exhibits are attached to and incorporated into this Agreement:

- **Exhibit A** — Legal Description of the Site (consolidated parcel)
- **Exhibit B** — Permitted Exceptions to Title
- **Exhibit C** — Community Center Specifications and Furnishing/Equipping Scope
- **Exhibit D** — Infrastructure Improvement Schedule (City off-site and Developer on-site)
- **Exhibit E** — Form of Performance Security and Draw Mechanics
- **Exhibit F** — Community Benefits Provisions
- **Exhibit G** — Form of Affordable Housing Restrictive Covenant
- **Exhibit H** — Project Budget and Sources and Uses of Funds
- **Exhibit I** — Phasing Plan and Site Plan
- **Exhibit J** — Form of Environmental Indemnity and ELUCs

[Drafting Note — Open items (Exhibits): The Term Sheet (Section 19) contemplates exhibits including a site plan, phasing plan, project budget, and form of restrictive covenant for affordable housing. Several exhibits required for a complete Agreement are not yet drafted and contain open items flagged throughout this Agreement: Exhibit A (legal description—pending survey and consolidation, and resolution of the county and parcel-size/orientation conflicts); Exhibit B (Permitted Exceptions—pending title review and Construction Lender approval, including the ELUCs and the affordable housing covenant); Exhibit C (community center specifications—pending agreement on scope, furnishing budget, and library component); Exhibit D (infrastructure schedule—pending agreement on City off-site completion timing and consequences of City delay); Exhibit E (Performance Security form—pending resolution of the dual-claimant conflict with the Construction Lender); Exhibit F (Community Benefits—pending confirmation of whether a standalone CBA is required); Exhibit G (affordable housing covenant—pending City and Construction Lender approval, including subordination); and Exhibit J (environmental indemnity and ELUCs—pending resolution of cost-overrun allocation and Construction Lender approval of institutional controls). These exhibits must be prepared and approved before execution.]

## SIGNATURES

IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the Parties have executed this Development Agreement as of the Effective Date.

**GRANITE CITY DEVELOPMENT LLC**

By: _______________________
Name: Marcus J. Pellegrini
Title: Manager and Chief Executive Officer

APPROVED AS TO FORM:

_______________________
Rebecca A. Sinclair, Esq.
Corporation Counsel, City of Lakemont, Illinois
Date: _________________

[Drafting Note — Open item (execution authority): The Resolution authorizes the City Manager (Gerald T. Fong) to negotiate and execute this Agreement, subject to the Resolution's conditions and approval as to form by Corporation Counsel Rebecca A. Sinclair and outside counsel Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP. Confirm that the City Manager's execution authority is sufficient (i.e., that no separate Mayoral signature is required) and that the Resolution's conditions have been satisfied or waived before execution. The Resolution was adopted by a 7-2 vote; confirm no subsequent Council action has altered the authorization.]

[Drafting Note — Consolidated index of cross-document conflicts and open items flagged in this draft:

**Cross-document conflicts (must be reconciled before execution):**
1. Reversionary interest / clawback — Resolution Condition 3(h) requires it; Loan Term Sheet strictly prohibits it (would prevent the Construction Loan from closing); Term Sheet silent; Lakeshore Side Letter requires consent. Resolved in this draft by eliminating the reversionary interest and substituting alternative completion assurance (Section 14.3). [CRITICAL]
2. 36-month reversionary trigger vs. Phase I Substantial Completion deadline (September 30, 2028) and groundbreaking deadline (March 1, 2026). [CRITICAL — tied to item 1]
3. Living wage ($17.50/hr for permanent retail/hotel employees) — Resolution Condition 3(c)(ii) mandates; Term Sheet silent. Incorporated in Section 9.2.
4. First-source hiring for permanent positions — Resolution Condition 3(c)(iii) mandates; Term Sheet silent. Incorporated in Section 9.3.

6. Equity demonstration timing — Resolution (60 days from February 18, 2025 = April 19, 2025, with Clearmont certification) vs. Term Sheet (60 days from City Council approval of the Agreement). Adopted Resolution's deadline in Section 6.2.
7. RFP number — RFP Response cites "2024-CD-07"; Term Sheet cites "2024-CD-018." Flagged in Recitals.
8. Parcel acquisition history — Term Sheet/Resolution/TIF Memo (Parcels 015 & 016 by tax forfeiture 2016; Parcel 017 by direct purchase 2018) vs. Phase II ESA (Parcel 015 by tax forfeiture 2016; Parcels 016 & 017 by direct purchase 2018). Flagged in Recitals and Section 3.1.
9. Parcel sizes — RFP Response (015 ≈ 3.8 ac; 017 ≈ 4.8 ac) vs. Phase II ESA (015 ≈ 4.2 ac; 017 ≈ 4.4 ac). Flagged in Recitals.
10. Parcel orientation — RFP Response (Parcel 015 eastern) vs. Phase II ESA (Parcel 015 northern). Flagged in Recitals.
11. Contamination location on Parcel 016 — Term Sheet (northwestern) vs. Phase II ESA (south-central). Flagged in Section 5.1.
12. Parcel 017 contaminants — Term Sheet (primarily lead and chromium, above residential) vs. Phase II ESA (only arsenic above residential; lead/chromium/barium below). Flagged in Section 5.1.
13. Insurance limits — Term Sheet (CGL $20M aggregate; no Professional/Pollution Liability) vs. Loan Term Sheet (CGL $25M aggregate; Professional Liability $5M; Pollution Legal Liability $10M). Adopted higher limits in Section 8.3.
14. Force majeure cap — Term Sheet (18-month aggregate) vs. Loan Term Sheet (180-day Phase I allowance). Flagged in Section 12.
15. Cure periods / lender cure rights — Term Sheet (60/90-day) does not address Construction Lender cure rights; Loan Term Sheet requires extended Lender cure periods. Added in Article 17.
16. Assignment / change-of-control thresholds — Term Sheet (50%), Loan Term Sheet ([50]%, bracketed), Lakeshore Side Letter (51%). Harmonized in Article 16.
17. Performance Security dual claimants — City (Phase I completion) vs. Construction Lender (collateral assignment). Flagged in Section 7.3.
18. TIF assignment / City subordination — Loan Term Sheet requires assignment of TIF rights and subordination of City interests; Term Sheet provides TIF subject to availability. Addressed in Sections 4.2 and 17.8.
19. TIF reachability / abatement interaction — TIF Memo projects $38M–$42M cumulative increment (with abatement) vs. $48M cap; abatement reduces TIF increment. Addressed in Section 4.2.
20. Land write-down vs. TIF cap — TIF Memo recommends specifying whether the write-down is charged against the TIF cap. Drafted as separate incentive in Section 4.1.
21. City infrastructure funding source vs. TIF cap — TIF Memo recommends specifying. Drafted as separate City obligation in Section 4.3.
22. Prevailing wage scope — Term Sheet ("all construction") vs. TIF Memo (broad statutory interpretation; Developer may resist private components). Flagged in Section 8.1.
23. Condemnation threshold — Term Sheet (Developer termination for non-City taking >15%) vs. Loan Term Sheet (default for any taking >15% by area or value). Flagged in Section 14.2(c).
24. County — Phase II ESA (Cook County PINs) vs. RFP Response (Lake County Recorder). Flagged in Section 3.1 and Section 18.4.
25. Affordable unit count — Term Sheet ("at least 100") vs. RFP Response/Resolution (106 / 20%). Adopted 106 in Section 10.1.
26. Phase I commencement triggers (internal Term Sheet inconsistency) — Section 13 (March 1, 2026 groundbreaking) vs. Section 15(c)(b) (6 months from conveyance). Resolved in Section 11.4.

Y. All Exhibits (A–J) pending drafting and approval.
Z. Construction Loan good-faith deposit ($250,000) and term-sheet expiration date (blank "May __, 2025") — Loan Term Sheet Section 14.
AA. Construction Loan Guarantors (Pellegrini + Pellegrini Family Trust) — completion guaranty and partial repayment guaranty terms to be determined (Loan Term Sheet Section 1).

## LAKEMONT STATION COMMONS — MIXED-USE TRANSIT-ORIENTED DEVELOPMENT

**by and between**

**GRANITE CITY DEVELOPMENT LLC**
(an Illinois limited liability company, "Developer")

**and**

**CITY OF LAKEMONT, ILLINOIS**

[Drafting Note — Status of this draft: This Development Agreement ("Agreement") is drafted from the Developer's perspective and is based principally on the Negotiated Term Sheet dated January 22, 2025 (the "Term Sheet"), as supplemented by (i) the Developer's Response to Request for Proposals dated December 16, 2024 (the "RFP Response"); (ii) City Council Resolution No. R-2025-017, adopted February 18, 2025 (the "Resolution"); (iii) the Preliminary Construction Loan Term Sheet from First Prairie National Bank (the "Loan Term Sheet"); (iv) the Side Letter from Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP dated February 10, 2025 (the "Lakeshore Side Letter"); (v) the Phase II Environmental Site Assessment Executive Summary by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC dated November 8, 2024 (the "Phase II ESA"); and (vi) the Corporation Counsel TIF District Summary Memorandum dated March 5, 2025 (the "TIF Memo"). Bracketed notes in the form [Drafting Note: …] identify (a) cross-document conflicts that must be reconciled before execution and (b) open items requiring further negotiation, diligence, or third-party consent. Defined terms are capitalized; terms not defined herein have the meanings given in the Term Sheet unless the context otherwise requires.]

---

**This DEVELOPMENT AGREEMENT** (this "Agreement") is made and entered into as of [•], 2025 (the "Effective Date"), by and between **Granite City Development LLC**, an Illinois limited liability company ("Developer"), and the **City of Lakemont, Illinois**, an Illinois home rule municipality ("City"). Developer and City are each referred to herein as a "Party" and collectively as the "Parties."

## ARTICLE 1. DEFINITIONS AND INTERPRETATION

**1.1 Definitions.** In addition to terms defined elsewhere in this Agreement, the following terms have the meanings set forth below:

"**Affordable Unit**" means a residential dwelling unit restricted for occupancy by households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of Area Median Income, as defined in Section 10.

"**Agreement**" means this Development Agreement, including all Exhibits and Schedules, as the same may be amended in accordance with Section 20.7.

"**AMI**" means Area Median Income, as published annually by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development for the Chicago-Naperville-Elgin, IL-IN-WI Metropolitan Statistical Area, or any successor designation.

"**Appraised Value**" means the as-is fair market value of the Site of $18,400,000, as established in the appraisal report of Crossland Appraisal Services dated January 15, 2025.

"**Construction Lender**" means First Prairie National Bank, and any successor construction lender or assignee permitted under the Loan Documents.

"**Construction Loan**" means the construction loan facility of up to $210,000,000 to be provided by the Construction Lender to Developer, as described in the Loan Term Sheet and the definitive loan documentation.

"**CO**" means a certificate of occupancy, temporary certificate of occupancy, or temporary certificate of acceptance issued by the City's Building Department for a building or improvement.

"**Conditions Precedent**" has the meaning given in Article 19.

"**Definitive Plans**" means the site plan, phasing plan, project budget, and form of restrictive covenant for affordable housing, together with such other plans and specifications as the Parties may approve under Section 2.4.

"**Developer's Counsel**" means Thornfield & Pratt LLP.

"**ELUC**" means an environmental land use control, in the form of a restrictive covenant recorded in the chain of title and enforceable by the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency.

"**Force Majeure**" has the meaning given in Section 12.1.

"**IEPA**" means the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency.

"**Lender-Approved Form**" means a form of this Agreement or any amendment, exhibit, or ancillary document that the Construction Lender has confirmed in writing is acceptable to it.

"**Loan Documents**" means the definitive loan agreements, mortgages, assignments, guaranties, and other instruments evidencing and securing the Construction Loan.

"**NFR Letter**" means a No Further Remediation letter issued by IEPA under the Voluntary Site Remediation Program.

"**Permitted Exceptions**" means the title exceptions set forth in Exhibit B, as the same may be approved by the Construction Lender under Section 17.4.

"**Phase I," "Phase II," "Phase III,"** and "**Phase IV**" have the meanings and components set forth in Section 2.2.

"**Project**" means the Lakemont Station Commons mixed-use transit-oriented development described in Article 2.

"**RAP**" means a Remedial Action Plan for the environmental remediation of Parcel 12-24-300-016, as approved by IEPA under the Voluntary Site Remediation Program.

"**Site**" or "**Property**" means the real property described in Section 3.1.

"**Substantial Completion**" has the meaning given in Section 11.3.

"**TIF Act**" means the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act, 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 et seq.

"**TIF District**" means the Lakemont Central Tax Increment Financing District established by City Ordinance No. 2019-42, expiring December 31, 2042.

"**TIF Reimbursement**" has the meaning given in Section 4.2.

"**VSRP**" means the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program.

**1.2 Interpretation.** The headings used in this Agreement are for convenience only and shall not affect interpretation. References to "Sections," "Articles," and "Exhibits" are to sections, articles, and exhibits of this Agreement unless otherwise stated. "Including" means "including without limitation." References to statutes include the rules and regulations promulgated thereunder and any successor provisions. The singular includes the plural and vice versa. In the event of any conflict between the body of this Agreement and an Exhibit, the body of this Agreement controls unless the Exhibit expressly states otherwise.

## ARTICLE 2. THE PROJECT

**2.1 Project Description.** Developer shall design, entitle, construct, and deliver the Project, a mixed-use transit-oriented development comprising approximately 1,620,000 gross square feet across four phases, with a total estimated hard construction cost of $340,000,000 and a total estimated Project cost of approximately $412,000,000. The Project components are summarized in the table set forth in Section 2.3.

**2.2 Phases.** The Project shall be constructed in four phases as follows:

(a) **Phase I — Residential Tower A and Retail Podium.** Three hundred twenty (320) residential units (256 market-rate and 64 Affordable Units), 45,000 square feet of ground-floor retail, and a 480-space structured parking garage, comprising approximately 420,000 gross square feet, with an estimated hard construction cost of $105,000,000.

(b) **Phase II — Office Building and Retail.** 280,000 square feet of Class A office space, 18,000 square feet of retail, and a 350-space structured parking garage, comprising approximately 380,000 gross square feet, with an estimated hard construction cost of $76,000,000.

(c) **Phase III — Residential Tower B and Hotel.** Two hundred ten (210) residential units (168 market-rate and 42 Affordable Units) and a 150-room select-service hotel, comprising approximately 440,000 gross square feet, with an estimated hard construction cost of $88,000,000.

(d) **Phase IV — Public Plaza, Community Center, and Public Parking Structure.** A 2.5-acre public plaza, a 28,000-square-foot community center to be conveyed to the City upon completion as provided in Section 2.6, and a 600-space public parking structure, comprising approximately 380,000 gross square feet, with an estimated hard construction cost of $71,000,000.

[Drafting Note — Open item (hotel brand): The Term Sheet and RFP Response state that the hotel brand affiliation is "to be determined," with Developer in discussions with two national hospitality flags. The Agreement should permit Developer to select the hotel flag in its reasonable discretion, subject to PD-TOD use compliance, without City approval of the specific brand. Confirm the City does not require brand approval.]
